narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—72% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. Picture your academic journey as the biggest chapter here: your Year 3 grades landed you in the bottom decile of your school, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward failure, echoing struggles that often carry over to bar prep. That low starting point in Year 1, also bottom decile, reinforced it, like a shaky foundation that made the model worry about building the stamina needed later. Your LSAT score of 30 added real weight too, as it's below the thresholds where most successful bar takers thrive, tipping the scales further against you. With a middle family income, the model saw less of the financial cushion that helps others afford extra tutoring or low-stress study time, making the climb feel steeper. Your Native American background, while a point of strength and pride, aligned in the data with higher hurdles for some in high-stakes exams like the bar. Even your solid 2.1 undergrad GPA and full-time student status couldn't offset these, as they whispered ongoing challenges rather than a clear turnaround. Gender played a small role too, but it was all these pieces together—your grades dipping low early and late, that LSAT hurdle, and the everyday realities of your background—that painted a picture of risk the model couldn't ignore. This isn't about your potential or heart; it's the model spotting patterns where students with this exact mix faced the bar and stumbled 7 out of 10 times. You've got time to rewrite this story—maybe retake the LSAT, boost those deciles with targeted study, or seek scholarships that level the income playing field. We're rooting for you to prove the odds wrong. Keep pushing.
